Adventure #3—Guys and Dolls
Adventure #4—No Use Crying Over
Stolen Milk
Adventure #5—Lights... Camera... My first visit to Tom’s garage had started with a loud noise. The second, with a mouthwatering aroma. This nextChange the Action
one began with an unusual visual impression: eight colorful wooden Russian dolls neatly lined up on a
Adventure #6—The Write Stuff workbench, each one just a little smaller than the next.
Adventure #7—Oh, What a Tangled
Web We Weave Previous Lesson Next Lesson	Mark as Complete
“This is a Russian ‘nesting’ doll—a matryoshka ,” Tom explained. “Each one fits inside the other. It’s a beautifulAdventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance
to Come Up with Great Ideas illustration of my sixth box: Put One Inside the Other . In engineering, it’s a great space saver. You put something
inside something else, or you nestle many things on top of each other—like a telescoping antenna, storing lead	Adventure #9—The Beginning
in a lead pencil, or folding chairs or paper cups nesting into one another.	Postscript... Four Years Later
“Here’s a little problem I confronted recently for an automotive manufacturer. They were at their wits’ end over—
of all things—cup holders. They wanted a container capable of holding anything from a small cup of coffee to a
huge Big Gulp or water bottle. But when it was snug enough to hold the small coffee, the big water bottle wouldn’t
fit. When it was big enough to hold the big water bottle, the small cup of coffee would bounce around and tip
over. They tried all sorts of flexible lips and grips, but nothing seemed to work.
“I went through a couple of boxes and quickly came to Put One Thing Inside the Other .”
“Ah,” I said, “you had them put the coffee in the big water bottle or the water in the small coffee cup,” I joked.
“That would solve the problem.”
“Indeed it would,” sniggered Tom. “In a way. But it was that kind of thinking that led me to the solution. I started
out with a large hole that could fit the biggest Big Gulp or water bottle. Then, I designed a series of smaller and
smaller cup-like inserts that fastened securely into the big hole and into each other—creating smaller and
smaller openings. The driver or passenger could create an opening that was just the right size for their drink,
simply by adding or removing inserts.”
I nodded, in admiration of his clever thought process. “And,” I said, “you never would have come up with that
solution if you hadn’t known about the Russian dolls and systematically tried to see if that idea would apply to the
situation.”
“Exactly!” said Tom, pointing his index finger in the air. “You know, nesting one thing inside another has been
applied to the bookstore industry with quite some success. Do you know what I’m talking about?”
“Putting a café within a bookstore?” I ventured.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 7 --

“Yes! What a brilliant idea that was—way back when someone had the thought to sell a cup of coffee in a
bookstore or a book in a coffee shop. What an effective symbiotic relationship: People who come for the
bookstore also spend money at the café, and people who come for the café also spend money at the bookstore.
“Of course, you’ve already got a café. But is there any other way you could use this box—any other way you
could nest one thing inside another—to help make your bookstore generate more profits?”
I thought for a moment. “I could do like the chains do and add a gift shop within the bookstore, offering book-
related and even non-book-related items. This would give people another reason to come in, and potentially
attract a younger crowd.
“And a nice little bonus is that there’s a much better markup on that kind of stuff than on books. I could even put
in some kind of supervised play area for kids. There’s nothing worse than trying to decide whether or not to buy
a book with a bored three-year-old tugging at your pants.”
“Good,” said Tom. “Now shift to another level.”
“Another level?”
“Yes. One of the beauties of these boxes is that they work at many different levels. You can use them to look at
things at a macro, ‘big picture’ level—or on a more detailed micro level. You can interpret the questions tangibly,
such as literally putting one thing inside another, or more metaphorically, such as by putting one idea inside
another.
“You’ve been thinking at one level: how to put some fairly big elements (such as a music or gift shop) inside the
entire element (the bookstore). Now, try shifting your perspective. What things can you put inside different parts
of the bookstore?”
“Putting things inside things…” I closed my eyes and imagined zooming in on my bookstore.
“Well, we’ve got plenty of books. What if I put something in the books? I keep reading that the more time people
spend looking at books in the store, the more they purchase. So, maybe I could put something inside the books
that people would have to look for—just some small pieces of paper or whatever. It could be like a treasure hunt!
And if people collect a certain number of the pieces of paper, they win something—like a discount or an
inexpensive bookmark.
“Putting one idea inside the other…” I continued, really getting a feel for this box. “That supervised area for kids
might not work out as an everyday thing—I’d have to devote at least one or two people to it full-time. But what if I
‘nested’ the idea of an invitation-only day or evening with supervised childcare on that day as a special reward
to my best customers. That could work.”
“That’s the idea!” responded Tom. “Keep moving around. Go from macro to micro—big picture to little. Specific
to general. Tangible to abstract. Go back to an earlier idea. Let one thing make you think of something else.
“In fact, would you like me to let you in on one of the biggest secrets of problem solving and of coming up with
ideas?”
“Sure…” I answered with trepidation. Though Tom had presented the idea boxes in easy-to-understand ways
until now, I couldn’t help but worry that his techniques might soon go beyond my reach.
“I can tell you in two words,” Tom announced dramatically, “The secret is: More, faster. ”
“More what faster?”
“Ideas! I’m talking about coming up with a large— huge —volume of ideas quickly , before your inner critic has
time to pass judgment. In fact, let’s try something right now. Give me a problem you need to solve. Anything.
Even something relatively mundane.”
“Well… our storeroom is always a mess,” I admitted. “I’ve tried and tried, but I just can’t get the team to keep it
neat or straighten up after themselves.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 7 --

“Good. Can’t get much more mundane than that.” Tom glanced at his watch, before handing me a pen and a
stack of paper. “Okay, next challenge: You’ve got exactly ten minutes to come with 100 ideas on how to solve
your storeroom problem.”
“I can’t come up with that many ideas!”
Tom raised his eyebrows again. Challenging me again. “Yes, you can—because I don’t care if they’re good
ideas or bad ideas. In fact, I want you to come up with some bad ideas. Not only do you have permission—it’s a
requirement . I don’t care if it’s an old idea or a new idea, and I don’t care whether it’s an idea you think will work
or one you think won’t.”
I sat back, resigning myself to my fate. Tom continued, “What we’re shooting for here is volume, pure and
simple. This is really thinking inside the box—a big box labeled 100 Ideas That Can Be Good, Bad or
Indifferent . And remember to keep your sense of humor, use the boxes and let one idea lead to another. Got it?”
“Got it,” I replied, feeling strangely free of any performance anxiety and anxious to see what my 100 ideas would
be.
“Ready? Set? Go!”
And off I went.
Here’s what my list looked like when I was done (at which point, Tom handed me a yellow highlighter with the
instructions to mark the ones worth exploring further):
Give prizes to whomever keeps stockroom neatest
Punish whoever doesn’t. Whippings?
Robot to clean room so don’t need people
Self-stacking books
Self-destructing trash
Self-emptying trash cans
No storeroom
Change my attitude about messiness
All books online
Paperless office
Eliminate need for shelving books
Trash all books as they arrive
Hire only anal-retentive employees (neat freaks)
Full-time person in charge of cleaning/shelving
Part-time person in charge
Intern in charge
Automatic stacking and shelving machine
Divide it more (more shelves, more subdivisions of where things go)
More trash cans
Bribes
Nominal cash reward or small prizes to everyone when I see neat storeroom
Give out candy when neat
Salary deductions when messy
New dimensions—sensory: Install good sound system with decent music so people will want to spend time
in storeroom. Turn off when not neat (carrot and stick).
Turn up heat in storeroom
Make storeroom more visually appealing (paint)
Nice smells in storeroom
Gamify keeping storeroom neat. Give points.
Prizes to whoever keeps neatest
Prizes to team who keeps storeroom neatest
Every day, a fun, 20-minute storeroom cleanup “blitz”
Take pictures of messy storeroom and display (humiliation factor)
Take pictures of neat storeroom and display (pride factor)
Make storeroom bigger
Make it smaller (less area to clean/mess up)
Turn storeroom upside down (put shelves on ceiling?)
Do the opposite (deliberately make messy? don’t store stuff in storeroom?)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 7 --

Divide storeroom into sections (make a place for everything)
Prohibit anyone from going into storeroom
Have only one person allowed to go in, so responsibility not dispersed
Fixed to movable (shelving on rollers? boxes easier to move? movable storeroom?)
Changing to unchanging (things don’t get moved around—everything stays where it is or gets thrown out or
shelved)
Remove what causes trouble. Don’t allow books in storeroom.
Less extreme: Don’t allow anything in storeroom that shouldn’t be there—books that should be shelved or
sent back to publishers, papers that should be filed, miscellaneous items that shouldn’t be there at all, etc.
Extract what works—put Jenny in charge of storeroom, since she does value neatness.
Bring things together: Combine lunchroom and storeroom (have to live with mess).
Combine my office with storeroom (so I can keep an eye on it).
As you can see, I didn’t quite make it to 100. But it’s a few dozen more ideas than I would have thought myself
capable of coming up with in such a short timeframe.
“Let’s see what we’ve got,” said Tom when my time was up. “I’m glad to see you’re taking advantage of some of
the boxes we’ve talked about. Hmmm, that one’s a bit weak, but not terrible. That one’s okay. That’s very funny.
This one might have some potential. And we could combine this one and this one…” And before I knew it, we
had three or four darn good ideas that I couldn’t wait to implement—ideas that never would have occurred to me
otherwise. And all in just ten minutes!
“I want to open more boxes,” I said, feeling like a kid at Christmas. “I can’t wait to see what’s in the next one.”
Tom seemed pleased with my enthusiasm. “Here’s a little something to take home with you. Would you like to try
to solve a mystery?”
“Sure.”
“A dairy that delivers its milk by tanker truck had a problem. Their customers—big milk processors—were
consistently complaining that they were a couple of gallons short.
“The company eliminated all possibility of error, leakage or evaporation. They had one driver and there was no
doubt that their driver was stealing the milk. So, they had private detectives follow the truck.
“The detectives watched him arrive at the dairy farm and fill up the big tanker truck. They tailed him as he drove
to the processing plants. They watched as the processing plants’ crews pumped out their milk from the tanker.
The driver never stopped en route. There were no visual clues as to how the driver had siphoned off or taken a
drop of milk from the tank. Yet, customers kept complaining they were short a few gallons.
“Now, here’s the mystery for you to solve: How was that trucker stealing the milk?”
Summary & Exercises
In a Nutshell
The box for this Adventure is the “Russian Doll” box, inspired by the Russian matryoshka dolls that fit one inside
the other.
The point is to make yourself look at things on different levels: macro (big-picture stuff) and micro (seeing the
details) levels.
Another way to use it is literally —for example, how might we literally place one thing inside another, like
bookstores put cafés inside themselves? Or on a metaphorical level: What is relevant that is like putting one
thing inside another? For example, can I place one idea inside another?
So really let yourself go with this one.
Now, let’s play with the exercises…
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 7 --

Click here to download this fillable PDF
Your Action Steps
1. Give yourself 10 minutes to come up with 50 ideas—good or bad––to solve your problem. Really do it. Now!
You’ll break through a creativity barrier and never look back. There’s a PDF you can download just down this
page. And room for his exercise in your Mindbook.
2. Any time you come up against a problematic situation or an opportunity, practice going from a micro (little-
picture) view of it to a macro (big-picture) view. Keep practicing this, and you’ll be less and less likely to miss the
forest for the trees. Or miss the trees.
3. Spend some time thinking about things being inside one another—at home, in your business, in nature or
wherever you find yourself. Think about how it would be if you took something out, put in something different and
so on. Have fun and play!
Catch you after you read or watch Adventure #4––about the mystery of the missing milk (and the Before and
After boxes)…
Come up with lots of ideas quickly to get past your inhibitions, resistance and fears, and to
ensure you’ll have plenty to choose from.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 7 --

Shift to another level. Go from macro to micro (or vice-versa)—from the big picture to the
smaller picture.
All of these resources are also available in your Idea Power Mindbook if you prefer to write
them out by hand.
Resources
50 Ideas in 10 Minutes
You’ll get your money’s worth hundreds of times over if you really USE the main concept of this Adventure:
uninhibitedly generating as many ideas and solutions as you can as quickly as you can.
Trust me… this really works. It will be like you’ve hired a team of consultants to assist you every time you need to
come up with a huge list of ideas.
So USE it. Really use it. Right now. Come up with dozens of ideas for something.
Anything!
Maybe it will help you get as excited as I am about this if I tell you a little about why pushing yourself to come up
with a lot of ideas—and I mean LOTS of ideas—works so incredibly well.
You see, most people have about the same ratio of good ideas to bad ideas whether they come up with 10
ideas or a hundred! So, by coming up with more ideas, you can’t help but come up with more good ideas. It’s a
mathematical certainty!
Next, just as importantly, ideas feed off each other. One idea—by having what we call “idea sex”—can give birth
to many others, thanks to the power of association. So-called “bad” ideas can lead to good ideas. Good ideas
can become great. So don’t hesitate to piggyback, extend, associate or simply take an existing idea and add a
new twist.
But perhaps most important of all, there’s the “Big Mo”: momentum. When you start coming up with ideas,
something loosens up in your brain, the cobwebs start to clear out, the barriers begin to come down and the
ideas start flowing more freely. I’m sure there’s a neurological explanation for it, but who cares? Tap into it and
reap its incredible benefits.
And remember: Never make judgments while you’re brainstorming. Write down bad ideas as well as good
ones. In fact, you should deliberately try to come up with bad ones. Keep a sense of humor––and remember the
battle cry: “More, faster!”
So why not, right now, come up with 50 ideas for that problem or opportunity you’ve chosen to tackle…
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
